These findings provide a detailed understanding of product-level performance and establish the foundation for the sales and profit analyses presented in the following subsections.

📊 5.6.1 Sales Analysis by Sub-Category¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to compare the total sales generated by each product sub-category.

By visualizing sub-category-wise sales performance, we can identify the products that contribute most significantly to the company's revenue and understand how sales are distributed across different product groups.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following horizontal bar chart illustrates the total sales generated by each product sub-category.

A horizontal bar chart is used because the dataset contains several sub-categories with relatively long names, making the comparison clearer and easier to interpret.

In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

bars = plt.barh(
    subcategory_summary.index,
    subcategory_summary["Total_Sales"]
)

plt.title(
    "Sub-Category-wise Total Sales",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Total Sales (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Sub-Category",
    fontsize=11
)

# Display highest sales at the top
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

# Format x-axis values with commas
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(
    FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f"{x:,.0f}")
)

# Gridlines
plt.grid(
    axis="x",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

# Add some extra space on the right for value labels
plt.xlim(
    0,
    subcategory_summary["Total_Sales"].max() * 1.12
)

# Add value labels
for bar in bars:
    width = bar.get_width()

    plt.text(
        width + 15000,
        bar.get_y() + bar.get_height() / 2,
        f"{width:,.0f}",
        va="center",
        fontsize=8,
        fontweight="bold"
    )

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

This visualization uses a horizontal bar chart (barh()) to compare the total sales generated by each product sub-category.

The chart is based on the precomputed subcategory_summary table, where:

  • The y-axis represents the product sub-categories.
  • The x-axis represents the total sales generated by each sub-category.

The sub-categories are sorted in descending order of sales so that the highest-performing products appear at the top of the chart. Value labels are displayed beside each bar to improve readability and allow quick comparison of sales performance across product groups.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Sub-category sales analysis provides a detailed understanding of which specific product groups drive the company's revenue.

Unlike category-level analysis, this product-level view helps management identify best-selling products, prioritize inventory planning, optimize marketing efforts, and allocate resources more effectively.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Phones generated the highest total sales among all product sub-categories.
  • Copiers and Chairs ranked second and third in terms of sales, respectively.
  • Bookcases and Storage also contributed significantly to overall revenue.
  • Labels and Fasteners generated the lowest sales.
  • Sales performance varies considerably across different product sub-categories.

🧠 Business Insights¶

A relatively small number of product sub-categories account for a large proportion of the company's total sales, indicating that customer demand is concentrated around certain products such as Phones, Copiers, and Chairs.

At the same time, several sub-categories contribute comparatively little to total revenue. These lower-performing products may require targeted marketing efforts, portfolio evaluation, or inventory optimization to improve their business contribution.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

Management should continue investing in high-performing sub-categories such as Phones, Copiers, and Chairs by ensuring adequate inventory availability and supporting them through effective marketing strategies.

Lower-selling sub-categories should be evaluated to determine whether improvements in pricing, promotion, product assortment, or inventory management can enhance their sales performance.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

A small group of product sub-categories generates a substantial share of the company's total sales, making these products key drivers of overall business performance.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis compared total sales across all product sub-categories and identified Phones as the company's highest-selling product group. The results highlight clear differences in product-level performance and provide valuable insights for inventory planning, marketing strategies, and product portfolio management.

📊 5.6.2 Profit Analysis by Sub-Category¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to compare the total profit generated by each product sub-category.

While sales indicate revenue generation, profit reflects the actual financial contribution of each product group. This analysis helps identify the company's most profitable products as well as sub-categories that require immediate management attention due to poor profitability.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following horizontal bar chart illustrates the total profit generated by each product sub-category.

This visualization highlights the company's most profitable product groups while also identifying sub-categories that generate low or even negative profits despite contributing to sales.

In [29]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 8))

bars = plt.barh(
    subcategory_summary.index,
    subcategory_summary["Total_Profit"]
)

plt.title(
    "Sub-Category-wise Total Profit",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Total Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Sub-Category",
    fontsize=11
)

# Display highest profit at the top
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()

# Format x-axis values with commas
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(
    FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f"{x:,.0f}")
)

# Gridlines
plt.grid(
    axis="x",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

# Add space for value labels
profit_min = subcategory_summary["Total_Profit"].min()
profit_max = subcategory_summary["Total_Profit"].max()

plt.xlim(
    profit_min * 1.25,
    profit_max * 1.12
)

# Add value labels
for bar in bars:

    width = bar.get_width()

    if width >= 0:

        plt.text(
            width + 5000,
            bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f"{width:,.0f}",
            va="center",
            ha="left",
            fontsize=8,
            fontweight="bold"
        )

    else:

        # Display negative value inside the bar
        plt.text(
            width / 2,
            bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f"{width:,.0f}",
            va="center",
            ha="center",
            fontsize=8,
            fontweight="bold",
            color="white"
        )

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

This visualization uses a horizontal bar chart (barh()) to compare the total profit generated by each product sub-category.

The chart is based on the subcategory_summary table, where:

  • The y-axis represents the product sub-categories.
  • The x-axis represents the total profit earned by each sub-category.

The sub-categories are arranged in descending order of profit to highlight the most profitable products first. Value labels are displayed for every bar, while the negative profit value for Tables is shown inside the bar to maintain readability and emphasize its loss-making performance.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Profitability is a more meaningful measure of business performance than sales alone. While high sales indicate strong revenue generation, only profitable products contribute positively to the organization's financial success.

This analysis helps identify the most profitable product groups as well as products that require pricing, cost, or inventory improvements.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Copiers generated the highest total profit among all sub-categories.
  • Phones ranked second in terms of total profit despite generating the highest sales.
  • Bookcases, Chairs, and Appliances also contributed significantly to overall profitability.
  • Tables recorded a negative total profit, making it the only loss-making sub-category.
  • Profitability varies considerably across different product sub-categories.

🧠 Business Insights¶

The analysis shows that high sales do not always translate into high profitability. Although Phones generated the highest sales revenue, Copiers delivered the greatest profit.

More importantly, the Tables sub-category generated substantial sales but resulted in an overall financial loss. This indicates potential issues related to excessive discounting, high procurement costs, or inefficient pricing strategies.

These findings highlight the importance of evaluating both revenue and profit when assessing product performance.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

The company should continue investing in highly profitable sub-categories such as Copiers and Phones by maintaining sufficient inventory and supporting them through targeted marketing initiatives.

Management should immediately investigate the Tables sub-category to determine the reasons behind its negative profitability. Pricing policies, discount strategies, procurement costs, and supplier contracts should be reviewed to improve its financial performance.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Profitability analysis reveals that the highest-selling products are not always the most profitable, and it identifies Tables as the only sub-category currently operating at a loss.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis compared the profitability of all product sub-categories and identified Copiers as the company's most profitable product group. It also revealed that the Tables sub-category generated an overall loss despite strong sales performance. These findings provide valuable insights for pricing decisions, cost optimization, and product portfolio management.

✅ Section Summary (5.6)¶

The Sub-Category Analysis provided a detailed evaluation of sales and profit across individual product groups. While Phones emerged as the highest-selling sub-category, Copiers generated the highest overall profit, demonstrating that strong sales do not always result in maximum profitability.

A particularly important finding was that the Tables sub-category recorded an overall loss despite generating substantial sales revenue. This highlights the importance of analyzing both sales and profit together when evaluating product performance.

Overall, this section identified the company's strongest and weakest product groups and provided actionable insights to support inventory planning, pricing strategies, and product portfolio optimization.

➡️ Next Analysis¶

Having evaluated product performance across categories, customer segments, regions, markets, and sub-categories, the next step is to analyze Shipping Mode Performance.

The following section examines how different shipping methods contribute to the company's sales and profitability, helping assess the operational effectiveness of various delivery options.

📊 5.7 Shipping Mode Analysis¶

📖 Business Scenario¶

Shipping plays an important role in customer satisfaction, operational efficiency, and overall business performance.

Different shipping modes vary in terms of delivery speed, cost, and customer preference. Understanding how each shipping mode contributes to sales and profitability helps businesses optimize logistics, improve customer service, and make better operational decisions.

This section evaluates the performance of each shipping mode using Total Sales and Total Profit.

🎯 Business Questions¶

This analysis aims to answer the following business questions:

  1. Which shipping mode generates the highest sales?

  2. Which shipping mode contributes the highest profit?

  3. Do faster shipping methods generate higher revenue?

  4. Which shipping mode performs least effectively?

  5. How can shipping performance be optimized to improve overall business profitability?

🎯 Overall Step Purpose¶

The objective of this section is to evaluate the performance of different shipping modes using Total Sales and Total Profit.

The analysis begins with a summary comparison of shipping modes, followed by separate visual analyses of sales and profitability. These insights help identify the company's most effective delivery methods and support data-driven logistics and operational decisions.

📋 Shipping Mode Performance Summary¶

The following table summarizes the performance of each shipping mode using Total Sales and Total Profit.

This comparison provides an overview of how different shipping methods contribute to the company's overall revenue and profitability before examining sales and profit separately through visual analysis.

In [30]:
shipping_summary = (
    df.groupby("ship_mode")
      .agg(
          Total_Sales=("sales", "sum"),
          Total_Profit=("profit", "sum")
      )
      .round(2)
      .sort_values(by="Total_Sales", ascending=False)
)

shipping_summary.style.format({
    "Total_Sales": "{:,.0f}",
    "Total_Profit": "{:,.2f}"
})
Out[30]:
  Total_Sales Total_Profit
ship_mode    
Standard Class 7,578,889 890,596.02
Second Class 2,565,747 294,161.06
First Class 1,831,067 208,104.68
Same Day 667,202 76,173.07

📝 Summary Table Interpretation¶

The summary table indicates that Standard Class is the company's dominant shipping mode, contributing the highest sales as well as the highest overall profit.

The remaining shipping modes contribute progressively smaller shares of revenue and profit, with Same Day generating the lowest sales and profitability. These findings establish a baseline for the detailed visual analyses presented in the following subsections.

📊 5.7.1 Sales Analysis by Shipping Mode¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to compare the total sales generated through different shipping modes.

By evaluating sales across shipping methods, we can identify customer preferences and understand which delivery options contribute most significantly to the company's revenue.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following bar chart illustrates the total sales generated by each shipping mode.

Since the dataset contains only four shipping methods, a vertical bar chart provides a clear and effective comparison of their sales performance.

In [31]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

bars = plt.bar(
    shipping_summary.index,
    shipping_summary["Total_Sales"]
)

plt.title(
    "Shipping Mode-wise Total Sales",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Shipping Mode",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Total Sales (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.grid(
    axis="y",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.gca().yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f"{x:,.0f}")
)

# Add value labels
for bar in bars:
    height = bar.get_height()

    plt.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height + 50000,
        f"{height:,.0f}",
        ha="center",
        fontsize=9,
        fontweight="bold"
    )

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

This visualization uses a vertical bar chart (bar()) to compare the total sales generated by each shipping mode.

The x-axis represents the different shipping methods, while the y-axis represents the corresponding total sales. Value labels are displayed above each bar to improve readability and enable quick comparison of sales performance across shipping modes.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Shipping mode analysis helps businesses understand customer delivery preferences and evaluate how different shipping options contribute to overall revenue.

These insights support operational planning, logistics management, and customer service strategies by identifying the most frequently used shipping methods.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Standard Class generated the highest total sales among all shipping modes.
  • Second Class ranked second in terms of sales.
  • First Class generated lower sales than both Standard and Second Class.
  • Same Day recorded the lowest total sales.
  • Customer purchases are heavily concentrated in Standard Class shipping.

🧠 Business Insights¶

The results indicate that customers strongly prefer Standard Class shipping, suggesting that affordability and acceptable delivery times are more important than faster delivery options.

Although premium shipping modes offer quicker delivery, they contribute a much smaller share of overall revenue, indicating relatively lower customer demand.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

The company should continue optimizing Standard Class shipping by maintaining reliable delivery performance and operational efficiency.

Premium shipping options such as First Class and Same Day should be evaluated to determine whether pricing, service quality, or promotional strategies can increase customer adoption.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Standard Class is the company's primary revenue-generating shipping method, while faster shipping options contribute comparatively smaller shares of total sales.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis compared total sales across different shipping modes and identified Standard Class as the company's dominant delivery option. The findings provide valuable insights into customer shipping preferences and support data-driven logistics planning.

📊 5.7.2 Profit Analysis by Shipping Mode¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to compare the total profit generated by each shipping mode.

While sales indicate customer demand, profit reflects the financial contribution of each delivery method. This analysis helps identify which shipping modes create the greatest business value.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following bar chart illustrates the total profit generated by each shipping mode.

The visualization compares the profitability of different shipping methods, allowing us to identify the company's most financially effective delivery options.

In [32]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

bars = plt.bar(
    shipping_summary.index,
    shipping_summary["Total_Profit"]
)

plt.title(
    "Shipping Mode-wise Total Profit",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Shipping Mode",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Total Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.grid(
    axis="y",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.gca().yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    FuncFormatter(lambda x, pos: f"{x:,.0f}")
)

# Add value labels
for bar in bars:

    height = bar.get_height()

    plt.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height + 5000,
        f"{height:,.0f}",
        ha="center",
        fontsize=9,
        fontweight="bold"
    )

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

This visualization uses a vertical bar chart (bar()) to compare the total profit generated by each shipping mode.

The x-axis represents the available shipping methods, while the y-axis represents the corresponding total profit. Value labels are displayed above each bar to improve readability and facilitate quick comparison of profitability across different delivery options.

💡 Why It Matters¶

While sales indicate customer demand, profit reflects the actual financial contribution of each shipping method.

Evaluating profitability across shipping modes helps the company assess operational efficiency, understand the financial impact of different delivery options, and make informed decisions regarding logistics and shipping strategies.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Standard Class generated the highest total profit among all shipping modes.
  • Second Class ranked second in terms of profitability.
  • First Class produced lower profit than Standard and Second Class.
  • Same Day generated the lowest total profit.
  • All shipping modes contributed positively to the company's overall profitability.

🧠 Business Insights¶

The profitability pattern closely follows the sales distribution, indicating that shipping modes with higher customer adoption also generate higher financial returns.

Standard Class is both the most preferred and the most profitable shipping method, suggesting that customers value its balance between delivery speed and shipping cost.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

The company should continue strengthening Standard Class shipping by maintaining reliable delivery performance and cost efficiency.

Management should also evaluate opportunities to increase the adoption of premium shipping options through promotional offers, improved service quality, or pricing strategies while ensuring operational profitability.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Standard Class is both the highest-selling and the most profitable shipping method, making it the company's most valuable delivery option.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis compared the profitability of different shipping modes and identified Standard Class as the company's strongest-performing delivery option. The findings demonstrate that customer preference for Standard Class translates directly into higher business profitability, providing valuable insights for logistics and operational planning.

✅ Section Summary (5.7)¶

The Shipping Mode Analysis evaluated the performance of different delivery methods using both sales and profit.

The results showed that Standard Class dominates all shipping modes in terms of both revenue generation and profitability, indicating that it is the preferred delivery option among customers. Second Class and First Class contribute moderate business value, while Same Day accounts for the smallest share of both sales and profit.

Overall, this section highlights customer shipping preferences and provides actionable insights for optimizing logistics operations, improving delivery strategies, and supporting data-driven business decisions.

➡️ Next Analysis¶

Having evaluated the performance of products, customers, regions, markets, and shipping methods, the next step is to examine the impact of Discounts on business performance.

The following section investigates how different discount levels influence sales and profitability, helping identify pricing strategies that maximize revenue while maintaining healthy profit margins.

📊 5.8 Discount Analysis¶

📖 Business Scenario¶

Discounts are commonly used to attract customers, increase sales volume, and remain competitive in the market. However, excessive discounting can significantly reduce profit margins and negatively impact overall business performance.

This section examines how discounts influence both sales and profitability, helping determine whether higher discounts contribute to sustainable business growth or reduce financial performance.

🎯 Business Questions¶

This analysis aims to answer the following business questions:

  1. How are discounts distributed across customer orders?

  2. Does increasing the discount lead to higher or lower profit?

  3. Are highly discounted orders generally less profitable?

  4. Is there an optimal discount range that balances sales and profitability?

  5. How should the company manage discount strategies to maximize business performance?

🎯 Overall Step Purpose¶

The objective of this section is to analyze the relationship between discounts and business performance.

The analysis begins by examining the distribution of discounts applied across customer orders. It then investigates how discount levels affect profitability, enabling the identification of pricing strategies that maximize revenue while maintaining healthy profit margins.

📊 5.8.1 Discount Distribution¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to examine how discounts are distributed across customer orders.

Understanding the frequency of different discount levels helps identify the company's pricing practices and provides a foundation for analyzing the relationship between discounts and profitability in the next step.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following histogram illustrates the distribution of discount values applied to customer orders.

The x-axis represents the discount percentage, while the y-axis shows the number of orders within each discount range. This visualization helps identify the most frequently used discount levels and highlights whether higher discounts are applied frequently or only in specific cases.

In [33]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

# Define discount intervals (bins)
bins = np.arange(0, 0.91, 0.1)

plt.hist(
    df["discount"],
    bins=bins,
    edgecolor="black"
)

plt.title(
    "Distribution of Discounts",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Discount",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Number of Orders",
    fontsize=11
)

# Set x-axis limits and ticks
plt.xlim(0, 0.9)

plt.xticks(
    np.arange(0, 0.91, 0.1)
)

plt.grid(
    axis="y",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

This visualization uses a histogram (hist()) to examine the distribution of discount values across customer orders.

The x-axis represents the discount percentage applied to orders, while the y-axis represents the number of orders within each discount interval. Custom bins are used to align the histogram with the discount ranges present in the dataset, providing a clear view of the company's discounting practices.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Before evaluating the impact of discounts on profitability, it is essential to understand how frequently different discount levels are applied.

Analyzing the distribution of discounts provides insight into the company's pricing strategy and establishes a foundation for assessing whether higher discounts contribute positively or negatively to business performance.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Most customer orders received little or no discount.
  • The number of orders decreases as the discount percentage increases.
  • Moderate discount levels are applied less frequently than low discounts.
  • High discounts (above 60%) are relatively rare and represent only a small proportion of total orders.

🧠 Business Insights¶

The distribution indicates that the company primarily relies on low-discount pricing to generate sales.

Higher discounts are used selectively, suggesting that they are likely offered during promotional campaigns, inventory clearance, or special business situations rather than as part of regular pricing practices.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

The company should continue applying discounts strategically instead of offering large discounts frequently.

Future pricing decisions should be supported by profitability analysis to ensure that higher discounts generate sufficient business value without significantly reducing profit margins.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

The majority of customer orders are completed with little or no discount, while high-discount transactions occur relatively infrequently.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis examined the distribution of discounts applied across customer orders and found that low-discount transactions dominate the dataset. These findings provide an important foundation for the next analysis, which investigates how different discount levels influence business profitability.

📊 5.8.2 Discount vs Profit Analysis¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to examine the relationship between discount levels and profit.

By comparing discounts with the corresponding profit earned on each order, we can determine whether higher discounts improve business performance or reduce profitability. This analysis helps evaluate the effectiveness of the company's discount strategy.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following scatter plot illustrates the relationship between discounts and profit for individual customer orders.

Each point represents a single order, where the x-axis indicates the discount applied and the y-axis represents the corresponding profit. The red regression line summarizes the overall trend between discounts and profitability, making it easier to identify whether increasing discounts generally increase or decrease profit.

In [34]:
plt.figure(figsize=(9,6))

sns.regplot(
    data=df,
    x="discount",
    y="profit",
    scatter_kws={
        "alpha": 0.5
    },
    line_kws={
        "color": "red",
        "linewidth": 2
    }
)

plt.title(
    "Discount vs Profit",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Discount",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.xlim(0, 0.9)

plt.xticks(np.arange(0, 0.91, 0.1))

plt.grid(
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • sns.regplot() creates a scatter plot and automatically fits a linear regression (trend) line.
  • The x-axis represents the discount applied to each order, while the y-axis represents the corresponding profit.
  • Each point in the scatter plot represents one individual customer order.
  • The red regression line summarizes the overall relationship between discount and profit.
  • scatter_kws={"alpha":0.5} makes the data points semi-transparent, reducing overlap and improving readability.
  • line_kws={"color":"red","linewidth":2} customizes the regression line for better visibility.
  • plt.xlim(0,0.9) limits the x-axis to the observed discount range.
  • plt.xticks(np.arange(0,0.91,0.1)) displays discount values at regular intervals.
  • plt.grid() adds light gridlines, making the chart easier to interpret.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Understanding the relationship between discount and profit is essential for evaluating the effectiveness of the company's pricing strategy.

While discounts can encourage customer purchases and increase sales volume, excessive discounting may significantly reduce profit margins. This analysis helps determine whether higher discounts contribute positively to business performance or negatively impact profitability.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Most customer orders are concentrated between 0% and 20% discount.
  • Orders with little or no discount contain the highest profit values.
  • As discount levels increase, profitable orders become less frequent.
  • High discount levels (50%–80%) show a greater concentration of negative profit values.
  • The regression line has a slightly downward slope, indicating an overall negative relationship between discount and profit.

💼 Business Insights¶

The analysis indicates that increasing discounts generally leads to lower profitability.

Although some discounted orders remain profitable, larger discounts are associated with a greater risk of losses. This suggests that discounts should be applied strategically rather than uniformly across all products.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Review products that frequently require high discounts.
  • Avoid offering excessive discounts on products with already low profit margins.
  • Implement targeted promotional campaigns instead of applying large discounts uniformly across products.
  • Continuously monitor the profitability of discounted orders to optimize future pricing strategies.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

The scatter plot and regression analysis indicate a negative relationship between discount and profit. While discounts may help increase sales, excessive discounting reduces profitability and should be carefully managed to ensure sustainable business growth.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

This analysis examined the relationship between discount and profit using a scatter plot with a regression line.

The results show that orders with lower discounts generally generate higher profits, whereas heavily discounted orders are more likely to produce losses. The downward trend of the regression line confirms that increasing discounts tend to reduce overall profitability.

These findings emphasize the importance of adopting a balanced discount strategy that attracts customers while protecting business margins.

✅ Section Summary (5.8)¶

The Discount Analysis examined both the distribution of discounts across customer orders and their impact on profitability.

The histogram revealed that most orders receive little or no discount, indicating that the company primarily follows a low-discount pricing strategy. Higher discount levels are used relatively infrequently and appear to be reserved for specific promotional or business situations.

The scatter plot with a regression line further demonstrated a negative relationship between discount and profit. While discounts may help increase sales, excessive discounting generally reduces profitability and increases the likelihood of loss-making transactions.

Overall, this section highlights that an effective discount strategy should balance customer acquisition with long-term profitability. Rather than relying on frequent large discounts, the company should adopt targeted promotional strategies that maximize both sales performance and profit margins.

➡️ Next Analysis¶

After evaluating how discounts influence profitability, the next step is to analyze the overall distribution of profits across customer orders.

The following section explores how profits are distributed, identifies the presence of profit and loss outliers, and provides a deeper understanding of the company's financial performance at the transaction level.

📊 5.9 Profit Analysis¶

📖 Business Scenario¶

Profit is one of the most important indicators of a company's financial performance. While sales measure revenue generation, profit reflects how effectively the company converts revenue into earnings after accounting for costs and discounts.

Analyzing the distribution of profits helps identify whether the majority of customer orders are profitable, whether loss-making transactions are common, and whether there are unusually large profits or losses that may significantly influence overall business performance.

🎯 Business Questions¶

  1. How are profits distributed across customer orders?
  2. Do most orders generate small profits or large profits?
  3. Are loss-making transactions common?
  4. Are there any unusually high-profit or high-loss orders (outliers)?

🎯 Overall Step Purpose¶

The objective of this section is to understand the overall distribution of profits across all customer orders.

This analysis helps evaluate the financial performance of individual transactions, identify the presence of profitable and loss-making orders, and detect unusual profit values that may require further business investigation.

📊 5.9.1 Profit Distribution¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to examine how profit values are distributed across all customer orders.

A histogram helps identify whether most transactions generate small profits, large profits, or losses, while also revealing the overall spread of profit values.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following histogram illustrates the distribution of profit earned from individual customer orders.

The x-axis represents profit values, while the y-axis shows the number of orders within each profit range. This visualization provides an overview of how profits are distributed across the entire dataset.

In [35]:
plt.figure(figsize=(9,6))

plt.hist(
    df["profit"],
    bins=30,
    edgecolor="black"
)

plt.title(
    "Distribution of Profit",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Number of Orders",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.grid(
    axis="y",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • plt.figure(figsize=(9,6))

    • Creates a figure of size 9 × 6 inches for better readability.

  • plt.hist(df["profit"], bins=30, edgecolor="black")

    • Creates a histogram of profit values.
    • bins=30 divides the entire profit range into 30 intervals.
    • edgecolor="black" adds black borders around each bar for better visibility.

  • plt.title("Distribution of Profit", fontsize=14, fontweight="bold")

    • Adds a descriptive title to the chart.

  • plt.xlabel("Profit (USD)", fontsize=11)

    • Labels the x-axis with profit values.

  • plt.ylabel("Number of Orders", fontsize=11)

    • Labels the y-axis with the frequency of orders.

  • plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

    • Adds horizontal gridlines to improve readability.

  • plt.tight_layout()

    • Automatically adjusts spacing to prevent overlapping chart elements.

  • plt.show()

    • Displays the completed histogram.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Understanding the distribution of profit is essential because it reveals how profits are spread across individual customer orders.

Instead of looking only at total profit, this analysis helps determine whether profits are consistently earned from most transactions or whether the business depends on a few highly profitable orders.

It also highlights the presence of loss-making transactions and prepares us for identifying profit outliers in the next analysis.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • The majority of customer orders generate profits close to zero.

  • The distribution is highly concentrated around small profit values.

  • A relatively small number of orders generate exceptionally high profits.

  • Loss-making orders are present and extend into the negative profit region.

  • The profit distribution is highly skewed rather than normally distributed.

  • Both extremely high profits and large losses indicate the presence of outliers.

🧠 Business Insights¶

  • The company earns modest profits from most customer orders rather than consistently large profits.

  • Overall business profitability is influenced by a relatively small number of highly profitable transactions.

  • Some orders result in significant losses, suggesting potential issues such as excessive discounts, high operational costs, or low-margin products.

  • Since the distribution is highly skewed, relying solely on average profit may not accurately represent business performance.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Investigate highly profitable orders to identify successful products, customer segments, or pricing strategies that can be replicated.

  • Analyze loss-making transactions to determine whether discounts, shipping costs, or product pricing are reducing profitability.

  • Review pricing and discount policies to minimize unnecessary losses while maintaining competitiveness.

  • Perform a detailed outlier analysis before making strategic decisions based on average profit values.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Most customer orders generate relatively small profits, while only a few transactions contribute exceptionally high profits or significant losses, indicating an uneven distribution of profitability across orders.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The profit distribution shows that the majority of orders produce small profits, whereas only a limited number of transactions generate extremely high profits or substantial losses. This uneven distribution suggests that profit is heavily influenced by outlier transactions. Therefore, identifying and analyzing these outliers is essential for gaining a more accurate understanding of business performance.

📊 5.9.2 Profit Outlier Analysis (Box Plot)¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to identify profit outliers using a box plot.

While the histogram showed the overall distribution of profits, it does not clearly highlight extreme values. A box plot summarizes the spread of the data and makes it easy to identify unusually high-profit and high-loss orders.

This analysis helps determine whether a few extreme transactions are significantly influencing overall business profitability.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following box plot visualizes the distribution of profit across all customer orders.

It displays the median profit, the interquartile range (IQR), and any observations that fall significantly above or below the normal profit range. Points outside the whiskers represent potential profit outliers.

In [36]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

plt.boxplot(
    df["profit"],
    vert=True,
    patch_artist=True,
    boxprops=dict(facecolor="skyblue", color="black"),
    medianprops=dict(color="red", linewidth=2),
    whiskerprops=dict(color="black"),
    capprops=dict(color="black"),
    flierprops=dict(
        marker="o",
        markerfacecolor="orange",
        markeredgecolor="black",
        markersize=5,
        alpha=0.6
    )
)
plt.xticks([])
plt.xlabel("")

plt.title(
    "Profit Distribution (Box Plot)",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.grid(
    axis="y",
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

    • Creates a figure of size 8 × 6 inches for clear visualization.

  • plt.boxplot(df["profit"], ...)

    • Creates a box plot to summarize the distribution of profit.
    • The box represents the Interquartile Range (IQR), containing the middle 50% of profit values.
    • The red line inside the box represents the median profit.
    • The whiskers show the normal spread of profit values.
    • The orange circles represent outliers (extremely high-profit or high-loss orders).

  • patch_artist=True

    • Allows the box to be filled with color.

  • boxprops

    • Customizes the appearance of the box.

  • medianprops

    • Highlights the median using a thick red line.

  • whiskerprops and capprops

    • Customize the whiskers and end caps.

  • flierprops

    • Formats the outlier points using orange circular markers.

  • plt.title()

    • Adds a descriptive title.

  • plt.ylabel()

    • Labels the Profit axis.

  • plt.xticks([])

    • Removes the unnecessary x-axis label to improve readability.

  • plt.grid(axis="y", linestyle="--", alpha=0.3)

    • Adds horizontal gridlines for easier comparison.

  • plt.tight_layout()

    • Adjusts spacing automatically.

  • plt.show()

    • Displays the final box plot.

💡 Why It Matters¶

A histogram provides the overall distribution of profit, but it does not clearly identify extreme observations.

A box plot summarizes the distribution using the median, quartiles, whiskers, and outliers. It allows us to quickly detect unusually profitable or highly loss-making orders that may significantly influence overall business performance.

Understanding these outliers helps businesses investigate exceptional transactions before making strategic decisions.

📈 Key Observations¶

  1. The median profit is close to zero, indicating that most orders generate relatively small profits.

  2. The interquartile range (IQR) is very narrow, suggesting that the middle 50% of orders have similar profit values.

  3. A large number of profit outliers are present on both the positive and negative sides.

  4. Positive outliers reach profits above 8,000 USD, representing exceptionally profitable transactions.

  5. Negative outliers extend below -6,000 USD, indicating a few orders that incurred substantial losses.

  6. The presence of numerous outliers shows that profit is highly variable across different orders.

🧠 Business Insights¶

  • Most customer orders contribute only modest profits, while a relatively small number of orders have a disproportionate impact on overall profitability.

  • Highly profitable orders may represent premium products, bulk purchases, or successful pricing strategies that should be studied and replicated.

  • Significant loss-making orders may result from excessive discounts, costly shipping, low-margin products, or pricing inefficiencies.

  • Because profit contains many extreme values, average profit alone may not accurately reflect the company's overall financial performance.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Investigate highly profitable orders to identify successful products, customer segments, and sales strategies.

  • Analyze severely loss-making orders to determine whether discounts, shipping costs, returns, or pricing decisions caused the losses.

  • Review pricing and discount policies for products frequently associated with negative-profit outliers.

  • Consider monitoring outlier transactions separately through business dashboards to support faster decision-making and risk management.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Although most customer orders generate relatively small profits, a limited number of extremely profitable and highly loss-making transactions have a significant influence on overall business profitability.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The box plot confirms that profit is concentrated within a narrow range for most orders, while numerous extreme positive and negative outliers exist. These outliers play an important role in shaping overall profitability and should be investigated separately to identify successful business practices and minimize financial losses.

✅ Section Summary (5.9)¶

The Profit Analysis section examined how profits are distributed across customer orders.

Using both the histogram and box plot, we found that most transactions generate relatively small profits, while a limited number of orders produce exceptionally high profits or significant losses. The presence of numerous outliers indicates that overall profitability is strongly influenced by a small subset of transactions.

These findings suggest that analyzing high-profit and high-loss orders separately can help the business improve pricing strategies, optimize discount policies, and reduce financial risk.

➡️ Next Analysis¶

In the next section (5.10 Correlation Analysis), we will explore the relationships between key numerical variables such as Sales, Profit, Discount, and Quantity.

By measuring correlations, we can identify which variables move together, understand the strength of their relationships, and uncover patterns that may explain business performance.

📊 5.10 Correlation Analysis¶

📖 Business Scenario¶

The company records several numerical variables for every customer order, including Sales, Profit, Quantity, and Discount.

Although each variable has been analyzed individually, business decisions often require understanding how these variables influence one another.

For example, managers may want to know whether higher discounts increase sales, whether larger quantities generate higher profits, or whether sales and profit move together. Identifying these relationships helps the business make more informed pricing, inventory, and promotional decisions.

🎯 Business Questions¶

  1. Is there a relationship between Sales and Profit?

  2. Does offering higher discounts reduce profitability?

  3. Do customers who purchase larger quantities generate higher profits?

  4. Which numerical variables are most strongly related?

  5. Which business metrics appear to have little or no relationship?

🎯 Overall Step Purpose¶

The objective of this section is to measure the relationships between important numerical variables in the dataset.

Instead of analyzing variables individually, Correlation Analysis helps identify whether two variables move together, move in opposite directions, or have little relationship.

Understanding these relationships enables better business decisions regarding pricing, discount strategies, inventory management, and overall profitability.

📊 5.10.1 Correlation Matrix¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The first step in correlation analysis is to calculate the correlation coefficients between all numerical variables.

A correlation matrix provides the exact numerical relationship between variables, ranging from -1 to +1.

This allows us to identify whether variables have positive, negative, or weak relationships before visualizing them using a heatmap.

📊 Table Introduction¶

The following correlation matrix shows the pairwise correlation coefficients among the numerical variables in the Superstore dataset.

Each value represents the strength and direction of the relationship between two variables.

In [37]:
# Create correlation matrix
correlation_matrix = (
    df[["sales", "profit", "quantity", "discount"]]
    .corr()
    .round(2)
)

correlation_matrix
Out[37]:
sales profit quantity discount
sales 1.00 0.49 0.31 -0.09
profit 0.49 1.00 0.10 -0.32
quantity 0.31 0.10 1.00 -0.02
discount -0.09 -0.32 -0.02 1.00

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • df[["sales", "profit", "quantity", "discount"]]

    • Selects the four numerical variables used for correlation analysis.

  • .corr()

    • Calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient between every pair of numerical variables.
    • Correlation values range from -1 to +1:
      • +1 → Perfect positive relationship
      • 0 → No relationship
      • -1 → Perfect negative relationship

  • .round(2)

    • Rounds all correlation values to two decimal places for better readability.

  • correlation_matrix

    • Displays the resulting correlation table.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Analyzing variables individually provides useful insights, but businesses also need to understand how different variables influence one another.

A correlation matrix helps identify whether variables move together, move in opposite directions, or have little relationship. These relationships support better decision-making in pricing, discount strategies, inventory management, and profitability analysis.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Sales and Profit show a moderate positive correlation (0.49).

  • Sales and Quantity have a moderate positive correlation (0.31).

  • Profit and Quantity have a very weak positive correlation (0.10).

  • Discount and Profit have a moderate negative correlation (-0.32).

  • Sales and Discount have a very weak negative correlation (-0.09).

  • Quantity and Discount show almost no relationship (-0.02).

🧠 Business Insights¶

  • Higher sales generally lead to higher profits, but the relationship is only moderate, indicating that profitability also depends on other factors such as discounts and product margins.

  • Customers purchasing larger quantities tend to generate higher sales, but larger order sizes do not necessarily produce higher profits.

  • Discounts have the strongest negative relationship with profit, suggesting that excessive discounting reduces business profitability.

  • Since discounts have almost no relationship with quantity sold, increasing discounts may not significantly increase purchase volume.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Continue focusing on increasing sales while maintaining healthy profit margins.

  • Review discount policies, especially for products that already have low profit margins.

  • Avoid assuming that larger quantities automatically result in greater profitability.

  • Monitor the effectiveness of promotional campaigns to ensure that discounts generate meaningful business value rather than simply reducing profit.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

Among all numerical variables, Discount has the strongest negative relationship with Profit, highlighting the importance of carefully managing discount strategies to protect business profitability.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The correlation matrix reveals several important relationships among the numerical variables in the Superstore dataset. Sales and Profit are moderately positively correlated, indicating that higher sales generally contribute to higher profits. However, Discount shows a moderate negative relationship with Profit, suggesting that excessive discounting reduces profitability. Overall, the analysis emphasizes the need to balance sales growth with effective pricing and discount strategies.

📊 5.10.2 Correlation Heatmap¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to visualize the correlation matrix using a heatmap.

While the correlation matrix provides the exact numerical values, a heatmap represents these relationships using colors, making it easier to identify strong positive, weak, and negative correlations at a glance.

This visualization helps quickly recognize important business relationships among the numerical variables.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following heatmap visualizes the correlation coefficients among the numerical variables in the Superstore dataset.

Each cell displays the correlation value between two variables, while the color intensity indicates the strength and direction of the relationship.

In [38]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

sns.heatmap(
    correlation_matrix,
    annot=True,
    cmap="coolwarm",
    fmt=".2f",
    linewidths=0.5,
    square=True,
    cbar=True,
    annot_kws={"size":11}
)

plt.title(
    "Correlation Heatmap",
    fontsize=14,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))

    • Creates a figure of size 8 × 6 inches for better visualization.

  • sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, ...)

    • Creates a heatmap from the previously calculated correlation matrix.

  • annot=True

    • Displays the correlation coefficient inside each cell.

  • cmap="coolwarm"

    • Uses a color gradient where:
      • Warm colors indicate stronger positive correlations.
      • Cool colors indicate negative correlations.

  • fmt=".2f"

    • Displays correlation values with two decimal places.

  • linewidths=0.5

    • Adds thin borders between cells to improve readability.

  • square=True

    • Makes each cell square-shaped for a cleaner appearance.

  • cbar=True

    • Displays the color scale bar indicating correlation strength.

  • annot_kws={"size":11}

    • Sets the font size of the displayed correlation values.

  • plt.title()

    • Adds a descriptive chart title.

  • plt.tight_layout()

    • Adjusts spacing automatically.

  • plt.show()

    • Displays the completed heatmap.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Although the correlation matrix provides the exact numerical values, it can be difficult to interpret quickly.

A heatmap transforms those numerical values into colors, allowing analysts and business stakeholders to immediately identify strong positive, weak, and negative relationships. This makes complex relationships much easier to understand and communicate.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • The strongest positive relationship exists between Sales and Profit (0.49).

  • Sales and Quantity show a moderate positive relationship (0.31).

  • Profit and Discount exhibit the strongest negative relationship (-0.32).

  • Quantity has only a weak relationship with Profit (0.10).

  • Discount has almost no relationship with Quantity (-0.02).

  • The heatmap clearly highlights that Discount is the only variable showing a noticeable negative relationship with another key business metric (Profit).

🧠 Business Insights¶

  • Increasing sales generally contributes to higher profits, but profitability is also influenced by pricing and discount strategies.

  • Excessive discounts negatively affect profitability, making discount management an important business concern.

  • Higher purchase quantities do not necessarily translate into significantly higher profits.

  • The visual representation confirms that pricing decisions have a greater impact on profit than order quantity alone.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Continue driving sales growth while maintaining healthy profit margins.

  • Carefully monitor discount strategies to avoid unnecessary reductions in profitability.

  • Evaluate promotional campaigns based on both sales growth and profit contribution rather than sales volume alone.

  • Incorporate correlation analysis into regular business reporting to monitor relationships among key performance indicators over time.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

The heatmap visually confirms that Discount has the strongest negative relationship with Profit, emphasizing the importance of balancing promotional discounts with overall business profitability.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The correlation heatmap provides a clear visual summary of the relationships among the numerical variables in the Superstore dataset. It confirms that Sales and Profit are moderately positively correlated, while Discount has the strongest negative relationship with Profit. The visualization reinforces the importance of maintaining an effective balance between sales growth and discount strategies to achieve sustainable profitability.

✅ Section Summary (5.10)¶

The Correlation Analysis section examined the relationships among the key numerical variables: Sales, Profit, Quantity, and Discount.

Using both the correlation matrix and the heatmap, we identified that Sales and Profit share a moderate positive relationship, whereas Discount has the strongest negative relationship with Profit. Quantity shows only weak relationships with the other variables, indicating that increasing the number of items sold does not necessarily improve profitability.

Overall, this analysis demonstrates that pricing and discount decisions have a greater influence on profit than sales volume alone.

➡️ Next Analysis¶

In the next section (5.11 Time Series Analysis), we will analyze how business performance changes over time.

Using monthly and yearly trends, we will identify seasonal patterns, sales growth, profit fluctuations, and important business periods that can support forecasting and strategic planning.

📊 5.11 Time Series Analysis¶

📖 Business Scenario¶

Business performance is not only influenced by products, customers, or discounts, but also by time. Sales and profits often fluctuate throughout the year due to seasonal demand, holidays, promotions, and changing customer behavior.

Analyzing sales and profit over time helps businesses identify growth trends, seasonal patterns, and periods of high or low performance. These insights support better forecasting, inventory planning, staffing, and marketing decisions.

🎯 Business Questions¶

  1. How do sales change over time?

  2. How does profit change over time?

  3. Are there any seasonal patterns in business performance?

  4. Which months perform the best and worst?

  5. Is the business growing consistently over time?

🎯 Overall Step Purpose¶

The objective of this section is to analyze business performance over time by examining monthly sales and profit trends.

This analysis helps identify growth trends, seasonal patterns, and recurring fluctuations that support better forecasting, inventory planning, and strategic decision-making.

📊 5.11.1 Monthly Sales Trend¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to examine how total sales change over time on a monthly basis.

By analyzing monthly sales trends, we can identify periods of business growth, seasonal fluctuations, and months with exceptional or weak performance. These insights help businesses improve forecasting, inventory planning, and promotional strategies.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following line chart illustrates the monthly sales trend throughout the available time period.

Each point represents the total sales generated during a specific month, allowing us to observe changes in business performance over time.

In [39]:
# Create a Year-Month column for monthly analysis
df["year_month"] = df["order_date"].dt.to_period("M")

# Calculate Monthly Sales
monthly_sales = (
    df.groupby("year_month")["sales"]
      .sum()
      .reset_index()
)

# Convert Period to Timestamp for plotting
monthly_sales["year_month"] = (
    monthly_sales["year_month"]
    .dt.to_timestamp()
)

# Plot Monthly Sales Trend
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_sales["year_month"],
    monthly_sales["sales"],
    marker="o",
    linewidth=2,
    markersize=5
)

plt.title(
    "Monthly Sales Trend",
    fontsize=15,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Month",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Total Sales (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

# Better date formatting
ax = plt.gca()
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(interval=6))
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter("%b-%Y"))

plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • df["year_month"] = df["order_date"].dt.to_period("M")

    • Extracts the year and month from each order date, allowing transactions within the same month to be grouped together.

  • groupby("year_month")["sales"].sum()

    • Aggregates the total sales generated during each month.

  • .reset_index()

    • Converts the grouped output into a structured DataFrame for further processing.

  • .dt.to_timestamp()

    • Converts the Period datatype into Timestamp so that it can be plotted on a continuous time axis.

  • plt.plot()

    • Creates a line chart to visualize how monthly sales evolve over time.

  • mdates.MonthLocator(interval=6)

    • Displays date labels every six months, preventing overcrowding on the x-axis.

  • mdates.DateFormatter("%b-%Y")

    • Formats dates as Jan-2011, Jul-2011, etc., making the timeline easier to read.

  • plt.grid()

    • Adds subtle gridlines to improve comparison between months.

  • plt.tight_layout()

    • Automatically adjusts spacing for a cleaner presentation.

  • plt.show()

    • Displays the completed visualization.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Business performance is rarely constant throughout the year. Monthly trend analysis helps organizations understand whether sales are improving, declining, or following seasonal patterns.

These insights support demand forecasting, inventory planning, marketing campaigns, budgeting, and long-term business strategy.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Monthly sales exhibit a clear long-term upward trend from 2011 to 2014.

  • Sales fluctuate considerably between months, indicating the presence of seasonal or market-driven variations.

  • The highest monthly sales are recorded during the second half of 2014, exceeding USD 550,000.

  • Early 2011 records the lowest monthly sales, with values close to USD 90,000.

  • Although temporary declines occur, the overall business trajectory remains consistently positive.

  • The business demonstrates stronger and more stable sales performance during the later years of the dataset.

🧠 Business Insights¶

The company has experienced sustained business growth over the four-year period, suggesting successful market expansion and increasing customer demand.

The repeated fluctuations indicate that sales are influenced by recurring seasonal factors, promotional activities, or customer purchasing cycles.

The exceptional performance observed during late 2014 suggests that the company effectively capitalized on high-demand periods, providing valuable opportunities for future planning.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Increase inventory levels before historically high-performing months to avoid stock shortages.

  • Analyze the promotional strategies and operational practices used during late 2014 and replicate successful initiatives in future years.

  • Investigate months with lower sales performance to identify operational challenges or opportunities for targeted marketing campaigns.

  • Incorporate monthly sales trends into forecasting models to improve budgeting, staffing, and inventory planning.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

The Monthly Sales Trend reveals a healthy and steadily growing business. Despite normal month-to-month fluctuations, the company demonstrates consistent long-term growth, with 2014 emerging as the strongest-performing year in the dataset.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The Monthly Sales Trend analysis indicates that the Superstore business has expanded significantly between 2011 and 2014. While sales fluctuate due to seasonal and market-related factors, the overall upward trajectory confirms sustained business growth. These findings provide valuable insights for forecasting, inventory management, and future strategic planning.

📊 5.11.2 Monthly Profit Trend¶

🎯 Step Purpose¶

The objective of this analysis is to examine how monthly profit changes over time.

While sales indicate business volume, profit reflects the actual financial performance of the company. Analyzing monthly profit trends helps identify periods of high profitability, loss-making months, and the overall financial health of the business. These insights support better pricing strategies, cost control, and long-term financial planning.

📊 Chart Introduction¶

The following line chart illustrates the monthly profit trend throughout the analysis period.

Each point represents the total profit earned during a specific month. By observing the trend over time, we can evaluate whether business growth is accompanied by sustainable profitability and identify months with unusually high or low financial performance.

In [41]:
# Calculate Monthly Profit
monthly_profit = (
    df.groupby("year_month")["profit"]
      .sum()
      .reset_index()
)

# Convert Period to Timestamp
monthly_profit["year_month"] = (
    monthly_profit["year_month"]
    .dt.to_timestamp()
)

# Plot Monthly Profit Trend
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

plt.plot(
    monthly_profit["year_month"],
    monthly_profit["profit"],
    marker="o",
    linewidth=2,
    markersize=5,
    color="green"
)

plt.title(
    "Monthly Profit Trend",
    fontsize=15,
    fontweight="bold"
)

plt.xlabel(
    "Month",
    fontsize=11
)

plt.ylabel(
    "Total Profit (USD)",
    fontsize=11
)

# Better Date Formatting
ax = plt.gca()

ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(
    mdates.MonthLocator(interval=6)
)

ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mdates.DateFormatter("%b-%Y")
)

plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.grid(
    linestyle="--",
    alpha=0.3
)

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

🔎 Code Explanation¶

  • Group data by year_month

    • Aggregates the total profit earned in each month.

  • Convert Period to Timestamp

    • Converts the monthly period into a datetime format for proper plotting.

  • Create the Line Plot

    • Plots monthly profit values using a green line with circular markers to clearly display month-to-month changes.

  • Customize the Chart

    • Adds a descriptive title, axis labels, six-month interval date formatting, rotated x-axis labels, and grid lines to improve readability.

  • Optimize Layout

    • Uses tight_layout() to prevent overlapping labels and ensure a clean presentation.

💡 Why It Matters¶

Sales growth alone does not guarantee business success. A company may achieve high sales while earning very little profit due to heavy discounts, high operational costs, or inefficient pricing strategies.

Analyzing monthly profit trends helps determine whether revenue growth is translating into sustainable profitability. It also identifies periods of unusually high or low profit, enabling better financial planning and strategic decision-making.

📈 Key Observations¶

  • Profit generally follows an upward trend over the four-year period.

  • Early months (2011) generated relatively low monthly profits compared to later years.

  • Several months experienced noticeable profit declines despite the overall upward trend.

  • Profit reached its highest levels during the second half of 2014, exceeding $60,000 in multiple months.

  • Month-to-month profit fluctuations are much larger than sales fluctuations, indicating that profitability is influenced by factors beyond sales volume.

🧠 Business Insights¶

Although the company is becoming more profitable over time, profit growth is not consistent across all months.

The sharp fluctuations suggest that profitability is heavily affected by factors such as discount policies, product mix, seasonal demand, and operational costs. This indicates that maintaining high sales alone is insufficient; controlling costs and optimizing pricing strategies are equally important for sustainable financial performance.

💼 Business Recommendation¶

  • Investigate months with unusually low profits to identify underlying causes.

  • Review discount strategies during low-profit periods to ensure profitability is not sacrificed for higher sales.

  • Replicate the business strategies, product mix, and promotional campaigns used during the highly profitable months of 2014.

  • Develop forecasting models using historical monthly profit trends to support budgeting and resource planning.

⭐ Key Takeaway¶

The business demonstrates a positive long-term profit trend, indicating overall financial growth. However, significant monthly fluctuations highlight the need for better control over pricing, discounts, and operational efficiency to achieve more stable and predictable profitability.

✅ Analysis Summary¶

The Monthly Profit Trend analysis reveals that the company has steadily improved its profitability over the four-year period. While profit generally increased over time, several months exhibited substantial fluctuations, suggesting that external business factors strongly influence financial performance. These findings emphasize the importance of balancing sales growth with effective cost management and pricing strategies.

✅ Section Summary (5.11)¶

The Time Series Analysis provides a comprehensive view of how the Superstore's business performance evolved over time.

The Monthly Sales Trend reveals a consistent upward trajectory from 2011 to 2014, indicating steady business growth and increasing customer demand. Although monthly sales fluctuate due to seasonal and market-related factors, the long-term trend demonstrates successful business expansion.

Similarly, the Monthly Profit Trend shows continuous improvement in profitability over the same period. However, profit fluctuates more significantly than sales, suggesting that profitability is influenced not only by revenue but also by pricing strategies, discount policies, product mix, and operational costs.

Overall, the time series analysis indicates that the business has grown steadily while highlighting the importance of balancing sales growth with sustainable profitability. These insights provide valuable support for forecasting, inventory planning, budgeting, and long-term strategic decision-making.

📊 6. Final Business Report¶

📖 Executive Business Scenario¶

Global Superstore operates across multiple markets, regions, customer segments, product categories, and shipping modes. As the business continues to expand, management seeks to understand the key factors influencing sales performance, profitability, customer purchasing behavior, and operational efficiency.

To support strategic decision-making, an exploratory data analysis (EDA) was conducted on historical sales transactions. The analysis examined business performance from multiple perspectives, including sales trends, profit distribution, discount policies, customer segments, product categories, shipping methods, regional performance, and time-based trends.

The objective of this report is to consolidate the analytical findings into meaningful business insights and provide data-driven recommendations that can support future business growth, improve profitability, and optimize operational performance.

🎯 Business Objectives¶

The primary objectives of this business analysis were to:

  1. Evaluate overall sales and profit performance across different business dimensions.

  2. Identify the most profitable and least profitable product categories and sub-categories.

  3. Understand customer purchasing behavior across different market segments.

  4. Analyze the impact of discount policies on business profitability.

  5. Evaluate shipping modes and their contribution to sales and profit.

  6. Identify relationships among key business variables using correlation analysis.

  7. Examine monthly sales and profit trends to understand long-term business growth.

  8. Generate actionable business recommendations that can improve profitability, optimize pricing strategies, and support strategic decision-making.

🔍 Overall Findings¶

The exploratory data analysis reveals several important insights into the sales performance, profitability, customer behavior, and operational efficiency of the Global Superstore business.

1. Business Performance¶

  • The business demonstrates a strong upward growth trend in both sales and profit from 2011 to 2014, indicating successful business expansion over time.

  • Although sales have consistently increased, profitability exhibits greater month-to-month fluctuations, suggesting that revenue growth alone does not always translate into higher profits.

2. Product Performance¶

  • A small number of product categories and sub-categories contribute a significant share of the company's total sales and profit.

  • Some sub-categories generate high sales but relatively low profits, indicating potential pricing, discounting, or cost-related challenges.

3. Customer Analysis¶

  • The Consumer segment contributes the highest share of both sales and profit, making it the company's most valuable customer segment.

  • Corporate and Home Office customers also contribute positively, providing opportunities for further business growth through targeted marketing strategies.

4. Regional Performance¶

  • Business performance varies across different regions.

  • Certain regions consistently outperform others in terms of both sales and profitability, indicating stronger market demand and operational effectiveness.

5. Shipping Analysis¶

  • Standard Class shipping accounts for the majority of total sales and profit, making it the most preferred shipping method among customers.

  • Faster shipping modes contribute comparatively less to overall revenue, suggesting that most customers prioritize cost-effective delivery over speed.

6. Discount Analysis¶

  • Discounts show a negative relationship with profitability.

  • Higher discounts generally reduce profit, highlighting the importance of maintaining balanced pricing strategies.

7. Correlation Analysis¶

  • Sales and profit exhibit a moderate positive relationship, indicating that higher sales generally contribute to higher profits.

  • Discount has the strongest negative correlation with profit, reinforcing the findings from the discount analysis.

8. Time Series Analysis¶

  • Monthly sales and profit both display long-term positive growth trends.

  • Seasonal fluctuations are evident throughout the years, suggesting recurring variations in customer demand and purchasing behavior.

  • The business achieves its strongest overall performance during 2014, reflecting continuous improvement throughout the analysis period.

💪 Business Strengths¶

Based on the comprehensive analysis of sales, profit, customer behavior, discounts, shipping methods, and time-series trends, the following key strengths have been identified:

1. Strong Long-Term Business Growth¶

The company demonstrates consistent growth in both sales and profit from 2011 to 2014. This indicates successful market expansion, increasing customer demand, and an overall healthy business trajectory.

2. Diverse Customer Base¶

The business serves multiple customer segments, including Consumer, Corporate, and Home Office customers. This diversified customer portfolio reduces dependency on a single customer group and contributes to stable revenue generation.

3. High Revenue from Consumer Segment¶

The Consumer segment contributes the largest share of total sales and profit, making it the company's strongest and most valuable customer segment.

4. Efficient Standard Shipping Operations¶

Standard Class shipping generates the highest sales and profit among all shipping modes. This suggests that the company's primary logistics strategy effectively balances customer satisfaction and operational efficiency.

5. Positive Relationship Between Sales and Profit¶

The correlation analysis shows a moderate positive relationship between sales and profit, indicating that business growth generally contributes to improved financial performance.

6. Consistent Regional Business Performance¶

Several regions consistently generate strong sales and profits, demonstrating successful market penetration and effective regional business operations.

7. Broad Product Portfolio¶

The company offers products across multiple categories and sub-categories, reducing business risk by diversifying revenue sources and serving a wide range of customer needs.

8. Healthy Business Expansion Over Time¶

The time-series analysis confirms continuous improvement in overall business performance, with 2014 emerging as the strongest-performing year. This reflects effective business strategies and sustained organizational growth.

⚠️ Business Challenges¶

Although the overall business performance is strong, the analysis identifies several challenges that may limit future growth and profitability if not addressed.

1. High Discounts Reduce Profitability¶

The discount analysis and correlation analysis reveal a clear negative relationship between discounts and profit. While discounts may help increase sales volume, excessive discounting significantly reduces overall profitability and affects long-term financial performance.

2. Profit Growth is Less Stable than Sales Growth¶

Although sales have shown consistent growth over time, monthly profit exhibits much larger fluctuations. This indicates that increasing revenue alone does not guarantee higher profitability, and operational efficiency remains an important concern.

3. Uneven Product Performance¶

Not all product categories and sub-categories contribute equally to business success. Some products generate high sales but comparatively low profits, suggesting pricing issues, excessive discounting, or higher operating costs.

4. Regional Performance Imbalance¶

Business performance varies considerably across regions. While some regions consistently generate strong sales and profits, others contribute relatively less, indicating opportunities for market expansion and performance improvement.

5. Dependence on a Single Customer Segment¶

The Consumer segment contributes the largest share of both sales and profit. Although this is currently a strength, excessive dependence on one customer segment may expose the business to future market risks if customer behavior changes.

6. Limited Contribution from Premium Shipping Services¶

Standard Class shipping dominates overall sales and profit, while faster shipping methods contribute comparatively less. This suggests that premium shipping services may be underutilized or not sufficiently attractive to customers.

7. Seasonal Business Fluctuations¶

The time-series analysis reveals recurring monthly fluctuations in both sales and profit. These seasonal variations may create challenges in inventory planning, workforce management, and financial forecasting.

8. Opportunity for Better Data-Driven Decision Making¶

The analysis indicates that business performance is influenced by multiple interconnected factors, including pricing, discounts, customer segments, products, and regions. Integrating these insights into regular decision-making processes could further improve operational efficiency and long-term profitability.

💡 Strategic Recommendations¶

Based on the findings of this analysis, the following strategic recommendations are proposed to improve business performance, increase profitability, and support sustainable long-term growth.

1. Optimize Discount Strategy¶

Implement a data-driven discount policy by reducing excessive discounts on products that consistently generate low profits. Promotional offers should be targeted toward products and customer segments where discounts produce measurable sales growth without significantly reducing profitability.

2. Focus on High-Profit Products¶

Increase marketing efforts, inventory availability, and promotional campaigns for product categories and sub-categories that consistently generate high profits. At the same time, review pricing and cost structures for low-performing products to improve their profitability.

3. Strengthen Regional Performance¶

Identify the business practices used in high-performing regions and replicate them in regions with lower sales and profit. Region-specific marketing campaigns and customer engagement strategies can help reduce performance disparities.

4. Expand Corporate and Home Office Segments¶
